## [00:00 - 03:00]

Now that you have your offer designed and created, you're probably wondering how to get it in front of your ideal audience. One of the easiest ways is through tapping into your existing network. Now imagine you've just launched a new online health and wellness class, specifically designed for homeschool moms. You want to incorporate more movement in their lives, wellness in their routines, you're bursting with excitement about this program, and you just can't wait to share it with the world. As you catch up with a friend, you mentioned your new class in passing. To your delight, she informs you that she's part of a community on Facebook that's for homeschool moms, and she's actually wants to get your name out there. Maybe she gives you the information for the group. She knows the admin personally, and this is where you can connect with the group space, and before you know it, you've gained your first clients. Alternatively, picture yourself casually chatting with your neighbor by the mailbox. During this friendly exchange, you mentioned your new fitness class and her eyes light up with interest. Recognizing a potential client, you decide to schedule a deeper conversation on how you could go over your coaching program, and the benefits for her as a stay-at-home mom. These are just some of the scenarios that can illustrate the power of leveraging your network. For you to find your ideal audience within a circle of people that you already know and trust. By tapping into these connections around you, you can quickly reach potential clients who resonate with your offerings and are eager to experience the benefits of your service right within your own inner circle. In our upcoming training sessions, we're going to delve into the strategies for harnessing the power of your network to attract and retain the clients within your industry. You'll learn how to identify and leverage existing connections, such as friends, family, neighbors, community groups, all to expand your client base. We're going to discover how to communicate your offerings in a compelling and relatable way that resonates with your target audience within your network. Let's explore some techniques for building the trust and credibility within your network, so you're positioning yourself as the go-to expert in your field. We're going to gain insights into effective networking strategies both online and offline, including social media engagement, community events, and referrals. You'll also learn to nurture long-term relationships with your clients within your network. That way, you're also providing ongoing support and value that keeps them engaged and loyal, and referring people to you. You're going to unlock the potential of your connections to grow your business through leveraging your current network while building your new network along the way. The most important thing, one of the biggest things you can do right now, is understand the importance of that warm network so that you can grow your business.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

Your network is absolutely key to the growth, but it's often neglected. Maybe we get too focused on something else that's taking our attention from our network we already know. The most successful people utilize their warm networks. Look at Tony and Dean. They have so many people within their network that they can call on and that they're referred to and they build out these amazing events and these amazing things together. So how can you authentically utilize your own warm network? To utilize that network, you want to first start with your connections. Your warmest network is filled with people that you have already established trust and that connection with. It's all about reconnecting with them. Show them that you care about them as a person and that it's not just about the sale. Sales is service. Dean always says that sales is service. I want you to think of the term service. Who can benefit from the value of your service? Who can you help? People are always worried about coming off as authentic, right? Sometimes we worry that maybe we're not coming off authentic because we know we want to help these people, but you also deserve to get paid for that service. This is why we approach sales through the lens of service. You'll only be concerned with how you can help them. Not so much about making the sale. The sale comes naturally, okay? Be genuine in your conversation with them. Trust in the social trust that you've already built up with these people within your network. It's important that you make a list of people in your warm network. Friends, colleagues, old classmates. This will be your starting off point for making connection calls. But what is a connection call? Connection calls are about 5, 10 minutes. This is not a sales call. Don't try to sell anything during this call. It's all about reestablishing that connection and informing them of your new business. The call is casual. There's two ways to ask on this call. There's indirect and direct. So an indirect way is saying, do you know anyone that might be able to benefit from my new service? Often they'll say themselves if they're interested. But it's a great way to get referrals. Second is the direct. My service is XYZ and I thought of you and the pain that you've been dealing with. See you don't have to make the direct ask during the connection call, but if you're in that call and it becomes the connectivity and you find that window to connect and ask them if they would like to move forward with service, allow yourself to have those conversations. Because listen, the answer may be no, but don't be discouraged. Maintain that connection because they could know people who might benefit from your service and that no might turn into a yes if you develop a new product or service that fits better
